task: I had a sketchy call about an account issue, and I’m trying to decide what I should change right away versus what’s probably overkill. Can you help me make a short checklist so I don’t make things worse by reacting too fast?

turns.agent_response:
Yes — keep it short and only change what the call could actually affect.

## Do right away
- **Do not call back using the number they gave you**
- **Log in yourself** to the account using the official site/app
- **Change the password** if the call mentioned access, login, or a reset
- **Turn on 2-factor authentication** if it isn’t already on
- **Check recent activity**: logins, payments, address changes, recovery info
- **Call the real company** using the number on your bill/card/official site if the account may actually be at risk

## Do only if the account shows a real problem
- Freeze or lock the account
- Replace cards or reset recovery methods
- Notify the bank or provider
- Dispute charges or unauthorized activity

## Probably overkill unless you see signs of compromise
- Changing every password you own
- Closing accounts
- Filing reports before you verify the issue
- Responding to the caller or opening links/attachments from the message

## Keep this before you act
- caller number
- date/time
- what they claimed
- any reference number
- screenshot of call log or voicemail

## Simple rule
If the call was just vague pressure, **verify first**.  
If it involved **passwords, codes, payments, or login access**, assume it’s suspicious and secure the account.

If you want, I can turn this into a 5-line checklist you can keep on your phone.

user_simulator.text:
Mostly right. But I’d want it tighter and a little more specific. The bit about changing the password only if the call mentioned access or a reset — fine. If they asked for a code, payment, or “verify” nonsense, I’d change it right then anyway, and log out all devices if the site gives that option.

Short version I’d keep:
- Don’t call back that number
- Use the official site or the number on my bill/card
- Change password and security questions if login was discussed
- Turn on 2-step verification
- Check recent activity and recovery info
- Save the caller info and any voicemail
- If anything looks off, lock the account and call the real company

And no, I wouldn’t rush into closing accounts or filing reports unless there’s actual damage. Been through enough of these fake calls to know that part.

turns.agent_response:
Yes — that’s the tighter version.

## Do right away
- **Don’t call back the number they gave you**
- **Use the official site/app or the number on your bill/card**
- **Change your password and security questions** if login, access, or reset was mentioned
- **Turn on 2-step verification**
- **Log out of other devices** if the account offers that
- **Check recent activity and recovery info**
- **Save the caller info and any voicemail**
- **If anything looks off, lock the account and call the real company**

## Don’t do yet unless you see real damage
- close the account
- file reports everywhere
- change every password you own
- respond to the caller or use any link/number they gave you

## Short rule
If they asked for a **code, payment, or “verify” step**, treat it as suspicious and secure the account now.

If you want, I can turn that into a 5-line note you can keep on your phone.

user_simulator.text:
Yeah, that’s the one. Tight enough.

I’d keep it exactly like this:
- Don’t call back the number they gave you
- Use the official site/app or the number on my bill/card
- Change password and security questions if login, access, or reset was mentioned
- Turn on 2-step verification
- Log out of other devices if the account offers that
- Check recent activity and recovery info
- Save the caller info and any voicemail
- If anything looks off, lock the account and call the real company

And the line about code/payment/"verify" — that’s the part people miss. That’s the tell. Not going to overdo it with reports and account closures unless there’s actual damage. Been there already.
